hints: Set x+y+z=25 with each ≥1 (ordered).
Stars and bars on positive integers: C(25−1, 3−1).
solution: We count ordered positive solutions of $x+y+z=25$. By stars and bars, that is $\binom{25-1}{3-1}=\binom{24}{2}=276.$

hints: Vertical bound: P(high K) − P(low K) ≤ (K_high−K_low)·Z.
Here 5.9−2.1=3.8 > (17−14)(0.9)=2.7 — the K=17 put is too expensive.
Sell P(17), buy P(14), buy 3 bonds: 0 Stock −1 Put(17) +1 Put(14) +3 Bonds.
solution: For puts, $P(K_{\text{high}})-P(K_{\text{low}})\le (K_{\text{high}}-K_{\text{low}})Z_0$. Here $5.9-2.1=3.8$ exceeds $(17-14)(0.9)=2.7$, so the $K=17$ put is overpriced. Arbitrage: sell the $K=17$ put, buy the $K=14$ put (net $+3.8$), and buy $3$ bonds ($-2.7$) to cover the at-most-$3$ payoff difference. Position: $0$ Stock $-1$ Put$(17)$ $+1$ Put$(14)$ $+3$ Bonds, locking in $1.1$ risk-free.

hints: The coefficient of C(k,3) is (15−k); write 15−k as a count and swap sums.
By hockey stick = C(16,5)=4368.
solution: The term with $\binom{k}{3}$ has coefficient $15-k$. Writing $15-k=\sum_{j=k+1}^{15}1$ and switching order, the sum is $\sum_{j=4}^{15}\sum_{k=3}^{j-1}\binom{k}{3}=\sum_{j=4}^{15}\binom{j}{4}$ (hockey stick), which is again $\binom{16}{5}=4368.$

hints: Cov = ρ·σ_M·σ_F.
An American call is always worth at least the European (more rights), and equal when there are no dividends.
Here American (3.15) < European (3.21): buy American, sell European.
Credit = 3.21 − 3.15 = 0.06, risk-free (you never early-exercise without dividends).
solution: An American call is worth at least as much as the otherwise-identical European call; with no dividends they're equal (early exercise is never optimal). Here the American ($3.15$) is cheaper than the European ($3.21$), so buy the American and sell the European for a $3.21-3.15=0.06$ credit. The payoffs match at expiry, so the $0.06$ is risk-free.

hints: Remainder 1 under 2,3,4,5,6 means n−1 is divisible by lcm(2..6)=60.
So n=60k+1, and also 11 | n.
60k+1≡0 mod 11 ⇒ k≡2 ⇒ smallest n=121.
solution: Since dividing by $2,3,4,5,6$ leaves remainder $1$, $n-1$ is a multiple of $\mathrm{lcm}(2,\dots,6)=60$, so $n=60k+1$. Also $11\mid n$: $60k+1\equiv5k+1\equiv0\pmod{11}\Rightarrow k\equiv2\pmod{11}$. The smallest is $k=2$, giving $n=121$ ($=11^2$).

hints: Exactly 2 teams advance, so the four contracts' payoffs always sum to 2.
Buying all four costs 0.84+0.73+0.25+0.15=1.97 but pays exactly 2.
Short 2 bonds (+2), buy all contracts (−1.97): risk-free credit 0.03.
solution: Since exactly $2$ of the $4$ teams advance, the four contracts always pay a total of $2$. Their combined price is $0.84+0.73+0.25+0.15=1.97$. Buy all four ($-1.97$) and short $2$ bonds ($+2$): you collect $2-1.97=0.03$ now, and at expiry the contracts pay $2$ exactly covering the bonds. Risk-free credit $=0.03.$

hints: Think in terms of distance still to travel up vs left; find the losing (P) positions.
The squares on the main diagonal (equal remaining up- and left-distance) are losing for the player to move.
The start has equal distances (7 and 7), so it's a P-position ⇒ go second.
solution: Track the remaining up-distance and left-distance to the top-left corner. A position with these two equal is a losing position for the player about to move: any move makes them unequal, and the opponent can always restore equality (mirror the move). The start needs $7$ up and $7$ left — equal — so it is a losing position for the first player. You should go $\textbf{second}$ ($2$).

hints: Tiny targets ⇒ maximize the sum of N(μ,4) densities at the two target centers.
With σ=2 large relative to the gap, the sum has a single peak at the midpoint of the targets.
Midpoint of −1 and 3 is μ=1.
solution: For radius $\varepsilon\to0$, the hit probability is proportional to the density at each target, so maximize $g(\mu)=\varphi\!\big(\tfrac{x_A-\mu}{2}\big)+\varphi\!\big(\tfrac{x_B-\mu}{2}\big)$. With standard deviation $2$ (large vs. the separation $4$), $g$ is unimodal with its peak at the midpoint of the targets, $\mu=\tfrac{-1+3}{2}=1.$

hints: Indicator per couple: paired with their spouse.
For any person, P(matched to their spouse)=1/17 (17 possible partners).
solution: Let $I_i=1$ if couple $i$ is paired. For a given person, the partner is uniform over the other $17$, so $P(I_i=1)=\tfrac1{17}$. By linearity over $9$ couples, $\mathbb{E}=9\cdot\tfrac1{17}=\tfrac9{17}.$

hints: Door d is toggled once for each distinct prime dividing it; it starts open.
It stays open iff ω(d) (number of distinct prime factors) is even (including ω(1)=0).
Odd ω: 35 prime powers (ω=1) + 8 numbers with ω=3 = 43; open = 100−43 = 57.
solution: Door $d$ is toggled once per distinct prime factor of $d$, starting open, so it ends open iff $\omega(d)$ (its number of distinct prime factors) is even (with $\omega(1)=0$). For $d\le100$, $\omega\le3$. Count odd $\omega$: $\omega=1$ are prime powers — $25$ primes plus $10$ higher powers $=35$; $\omega=3$ are $\{30,60,90,42,84,66,78,70\}=8$. So $43$ doors close and $100-43=57$ stay open.

hints: By symmetry P(you higher)=P(dealer higher); the rest is a tie.
P(tie in rank)=3/51=1/17.
solution: By symmetry $P(\text{you higher})=P(\text{dealer higher})$, and the remaining probability is a rank tie. Given your card, $3$ of the other $51$ share its rank, so $P(\text{tie})=\tfrac3{51}=\tfrac1{17}$. Hence $P(\text{win})=\tfrac{1-1/17}{2}=\tfrac{8}{17}.$
